name: Hip Dysplasia
summary: A malformation of the hip joint where the ball and socket don't fit or develop properly, common in large and giant breed dogs.
symptoms: Reluctance to jump or climb stairs
generalInfo: Hip dysplasia is largely genetic but can be worsened by rapid growth, excess weight, or high-impact exercise in puppyhood. Diagnosis is typically made via physical exam and X-ray. Management ranges from weight control and joint supplements for mild cases to surgery for severe cases.

name: Elbow Dysplasia
summary: An abnormal development of the elbow joint, one of the most common causes of front-leg lameness in large-breed dogs.
symptoms: Reluctance to extend the elbow fully
generalInfo: Often appears in dogs under two years old. Early diagnosis via X-ray or CT can help manage progression through weight control, physical therapy, or surgery in more severe cases.

name: Brachycephalic Airway Syndrome
summary: A set of upper airway abnormalities common in flat-faced breeds that can cause breathing difficulty, especially in heat or during exercise.
generalInfo: Severity varies widely. Owners of affected breeds are generally advised to avoid strenuous exercise in heat and monitor breathing closely; surgical correction is available for moderate to severe cases.

name: Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD)
summary: A spinal condition where the cushioning discs between vertebrae bulge or rupture, common in long-backed breeds.
symptoms: Yelping when touched or picked up
generalInfo: IVDD is considered a veterinary emergency if hind-leg function is affected. Owners of predisposed breeds are often advised to limit jumping and use ramps instead of stairs.

name: Mitral Valve Disease
summary: A progressive heart condition where the mitral valve degenerates over time, one of the most common heart diseases in small-breed dogs.
generalInfo: Often detected via a heart murmur at a routine vet visit before symptoms appear, which is why regular checkups matter for predisposed breeds.

name: Bloat (Gastric Dilatation-Volvulus)
summary: A life-threatening emergency where the stomach fills with gas and can twist, cutting off blood supply.
generalInfo: This is a true emergency requiring immediate veterinary care -- it can be fatal within hours. Deep-chested large breeds are at higher risk, and many vets recommend slower feeding and avoiding vigorous exercise right after meals.

name: Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM)
summary: A thickening of the heart muscle and the most common heart disease in cats.
generalInfo: HCM can be silent for years and is sometimes first detected via a heart murmur or on imaging. Genetic testing exists for some predisposed breeds like Maine Coons and Ragdolls.

name: Polycystic Kidney Disease (PKD)
summary: An inherited condition causing fluid-filled cysts to form in the kidneys, gradually reducing kidney function.
generalInfo: PKD is typically diagnosed via ultrasound and is most associated with Persian and related breeds. There is no cure, but management focuses on supporting kidney function and monitoring.

name: Dental Disease
summary: One of the most common health issues in pets, especially small dog breeds, ranging from plaque buildup to periodontal disease.
symptoms: Yellow or brown tartar on teeth
generalInfo: Regular tooth brushing and professional cleanings are the main preventive measures. Left untreated, dental disease can lead to tooth loss and has been linked to broader health issues.

name: Obesity
summary: Excess body weight is one of the most common preventable health problems in pets and a risk factor for many other conditions.
generalInfo: Obesity increases risk for joint problems, diabetes, and heart strain. Vets typically recommend a measured feeding plan and regular exercise tailored to the individual pet.

name: Ear Infections
summary: Inflammation or infection of the ear canal, especially common in breeds with floppy ears or heavy ear hair.
generalInfo: Regular ear cleaning and drying after swimming can help prevent infections in predisposed breeds. Persistent infections should be evaluated by a vet, as they can indicate allergies or other underlying issues.

name: Patellar Luxation
summary: A kneecap that slips out of its normal position, common in small and toy dog breeds.
symptoms: Holding a leg up briefly while walking
generalInfo: Mild cases may not need treatment, while more severe or frequent luxations are often corrected surgically. A vet can grade the severity on a 1-4 scale.

name: Epilepsy
summary: A neurological condition causing recurrent seizures, with a genetic (idiopathic) form common in several breeds.
symptoms: Sometimes drooling or paddling motions during a seizure
generalInfo: Idiopathic epilepsy usually appears between 6 months and 6 years of age. Diagnosis involves ruling out other causes, and many dogs are well managed long-term with anti-seizure medication.

name: Collie Eye Anomaly
summary: An inherited eye condition affecting development of the retina and other eye structures, found mainly in herding breeds.
symptoms: Often no visible symptoms in mild cases
generalInfo: This condition is typically detected via an eye exam by a veterinary ophthalmologist in puppyhood, since it's present from birth and doesn't worsen over time in most cases.

name: Skin Allergies
summary: An overreaction of the immune system to environmental, food, or flea allergens, resulting in itchy, inflamed skin.
generalInfo: Identifying the trigger often requires a process of elimination with a vet's guidance, including diet trials or allergy testing. Management may include medicated shampoos, dietary changes, or medication.
